narrative_summary:
The POLE A252V (p.Ala252Val) missense change occurs in the exonuclease domain of the DNA polymerase epsilon catalytic subunit and has been reported in a variety of tumor types, including colorectal, endometrial, lung, breast, and gastric cancers. Early tumor sequencing of sporadic colorectal cancers found the variant at a frequency comparable to that in the general population, suggesting it may represent a common polymorphism rather than a driver mutation [PMC4310866].

Clinical observations are heterogeneous. A germline A252V case was identified in a phase‑II trial of toripalimab in advanced solid tumours, but the report provided no outcome or pathogenicity assessment for the variant [PMC11366758]. In a review of DNA‑replication fidelity, the same variant was linked to reduced overall survival in lung cancer patients, implying a possible adverse clinical effect [PMC2993855]. High‑throughput sequencing of human iPSC lines classified A252V as a high‑ or moderate‑impact mutation present in the COSMIC database, although the derived iPSC tumours were not malignant [PMC6171902]. In the NCI‑60 cell line panel, A252V was significantly associated with altered sensitivity to natural product compounds, indicating a functional impact on drug response [PMC7858122]. Computational annotation in an endometrial cancer dataset predicted the variant to be deleterious (SIFT score 0.01, PolyPhen‑2 probably damaging) and to have low functional impact by MutationAssessor, supporting a pathogenic interpretation [PMC11312386]. Conversely, a breast‑cancer susceptibility consortium found no significant association between A252V and breast cancer risk (odds ratios ~1, confidence intervals crossing 1) [PMC4204770], and a Chinese breast/ovarian cancer cohort annotated the variant as benign in ClinSig [PMC6439399]. Additional tumor sequencing studies in NSCLC, intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, colorectal cancer cell lines, and bladder cancer organoids reported the presence of A252V without any claim of pathogenicity [PMC8787147, PMC5940669, PMC7176734, PMC10844626].

Functional data are limited to in silico predictions and association studies. The variant is listed as high/moderate impact in COSMIC and shows deleterious computational scores, but no direct biochemical assays of polymerase activity or proofreading fidelity have been reported. The NCI‑60 drug‑response association provides indirect functional evidence of altered cellular phenotype.

Population‑level analyses consistently show that A252V occurs at a frequency similar to that of the general population and does not confer a measurable increase in breast or gastric cancer risk, reinforcing the view that it may be a common polymorphism [PMC4310866, PMC4204770, PMC5482622].

Taken together, the evidence is conflicting: some studies suggest a possible adverse clinical impact and functional relevance, while larger population and several cancer‑specific cohorts treat the variant as benign or of uncertain significance. The preponderance of data points to A252V being a relatively common POLE allele with no clear, consistent pathogenic effect, and its classification remains uncertain pending functional validation and larger clinical correlation studies.

mentions.quoted_text: Inspired by the association of Polε exonuclease domain mutations with intestinal cancer in mice (2) and humans (5, 8, 9), we began this study by analyzing this region of Polε in 52 sporadic CRC patients treated at UNMC. The age distribution of the patients and the anatomical site of the tumor are listed in Table 1. Ten of the 52 tumors carried a common variant allele producing an **Ala252Val** change in Polε (nine in heterozygous and one in homozygous state; Table 1). The variant allele frequency did not differ significantly (p=0.11; Chi-square) from that reported for the general population (16). One tumor carried an Arg259His variant, also observed previously in the general population (allele frequency 0.5-1%). Remarkably, one tumor (T-04-364) had a heterozygous mutation leading to a P286R substitution in the ExoI motif (Table 1; Fig. 1). This change was not present in the publicly available databases of common polymorphisms, but was reported previously as a somatic mutation in 16 other sporadic cancer cases (6, 7, 9) and as a potential cancer-specific variant in a colon carcinoma cell line HCC2998 (10). Other mutations affecting Pro286 in Polε or a homologous Pro327 in Polδ were also observed in CRC patients (5, 8). The P286R change found in tumor T-04-364 was not present in normal cells of the patient (Fig. 1A), indicating that it was a somatic mutation. The recurrent emergence of Pro286 alterations is in sharp contrast to the apparently random incidence in tumors of other changes in the exonuclease domain of Polε, most of which were only observed once (5-7, 9).

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study notes that the POLE Ala252Val variant is a common allele observed in colorectal tumors at a frequency comparable to the general population, suggesting it is likely a benign polymorphism rather than a cancer‑causing mutation.
article_mentions.title: A common cancer-associated DNA polymerase ε mutation causes an exceptionally strong mutator phenotype, indicating fidelity defects distinct from loss of proofreading

mentions.quoted_text: POLE/POLD1 variants are detailed in Table 2 and Fig. 3. Of all 15 patients, ten had POLE mutation (one **A252V** variant was germline), four had POLD1 mutation (one R682Q variant was germline), and one had malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor with both POLE and POLD1 mutations. Most patients had one POLE or POLD1 mutation, while three had more than one mutation: one with S297F and S1906Y POLE mutations, one with P286R and F1907L POLE mutations, one with L523-L526del POLD1 mutation, I2070-K2072del and T2273-E2275del POLE mutations. Among three patients with more than one mutation, two had an objective response, and one did not undergo a CT scan after one dose of toripalimab.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The phase II trial of toripalimab in advanced solid tumors with POLE/POLD1 mutations notes a single germline POLE A252V case but provides no specific pathogenicity or therapeutic response information for this variant.
article_mentions.title: A phase II clinical trial of toripalimab in advanced solid tumors with polymerase epsilon/polymerase delta (POLE/POLD1) mutation

mentions.quoted_text: There are relatively few studies examining the cancer risk associated with non-synonymous SNPS in POLD1 or POLE. POLE E2140K has been associated with an increased risk of breast cancer [207], but not cervical cancer [208]; **A252V** is associated with decreased overall survival in lung cancer patients [209]; and N1396S is not associated with meningioma risk [210]. The POLD1 R119H SNP has been reported in multiple genome-wide SNP studies, and no association was detected with meningioma, bladder or breast cancer risk [210–213]. Surprisingly, most studies looking for associations with increased cancer risk have studied POLD1 or POLE intronic or synonymous coding SNPs that are unlikely to affect polymerase expression or fidelity.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article notes that the POLE A252V variant is linked to reduced overall survival in lung cancer patients, suggesting a potentially deleterious clinical effect.
article_mentions.title: DNA Replication Fidelity and Cancer

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study shows that a custom 156‑gene NGS panel can detect POLE mutations associated with high tumor mutational burden in endometrial cancer, but sequencing alone cannot fully assign tumors to the TCGA molecular subgroups.
article_mentions.title: Utility of a custom designed next generation DNA sequencing gene panel to molecularly classify endometrial cancers according to The Cancer Genome Atlas subgroups

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study reports that the POLE A252V variant shows no significant association with breast cancer risk (odds ratios ≈1, confidence intervals crossing 1).',
article_mentions.title: Common non-synonymous SNPs associated with breast cancer susceptibility: findings from the Breast Cancer Association Consortium

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identified the POLE A252V missense variant in hiPSC lines and classified it as a high‑ or moderate‑impact mutation present in the COSMIC cancer database, though hiPSC‑derived tumors showed no malignancy.
article_mentions.title: Tumorigenicity-associated characteristics of human iPS cell lines

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study shows that comprehensive genomic profiling across germline, tissue, and liquid biopsy can detect variants such as POLE A252V, but the clinical significance of this particular variant remains uncertain.
article_mentions.title: Combining germline, tissue and liquid biopsy analysis by comprehensive genomic profiling to improve the yield of actionable variants in a real-world cancer cohort

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study screened DNA‑repair gene polymorphisms for gastric cancer risk and included POLE A252V among the SNPs examined, but no association or pathogenicity interpretation for this variant was reported.
article_mentions.title: Relevance of DNA repair gene polymorphisms to gastric cancer risk and phenotype

article_mentions.title: Urine-derived bladder cancer organoids (urinoids) as a tool for cancer longitudinal response monitoring and therapy adaptation

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study focuses on FANCC germline mutations in Chinese breast/ovarian cancer patients and, in a supplementary table, reports the POLE A252V variant as benign.
article_mentions.title: Deleterious Mutations in DNA Repair Gene FANCC Exist in BRCA1/2-Negative Chinese Familial Breast and/or Ovarian Cancer Patients

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study characterizes DCHS1 as a novel biomarker associated with immune infiltration, EMT, and prognosis in endometrial cancer, while also reporting a POLE A252V variant with deleterious computational predictions in supplementary data.
article_mentions.title: Dachsous cadherin related 1 (DCHS1) is a novel biomarker for immune infiltration and epithelial-mesenchymal transition in endometrial cancer via pan-cancer analysis

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study compares different tumor sampling and pooling strategies for whole‑exome sequencing and concludes that a single high‑coverage run per sample is the most practical approach for clinical use.
article_mentions.title: Currently favored sampling practices for tumor sequencing can produce optimal results in the clinical setting
